title: Water on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Water stains are mineral deposits (calcium, magnesium, iron) left behind when water evaporates from upholstery. Hard water leaves white/chalky residue. Iron-rich water leaves rust-colored marks. The minerals bond to fiber through electrostatic interactions and can etch certain surfaces (marble, glass) if left in contact. The 'stain' is actually a deposit, not absorbed color.
whyThisWorks: A mild acid (white vinegar or citric acid) dissolves the mineral deposits on upholstery by converting them to soluble salts. For iron-based water stains, a reducing agent (oxalic acid) is more effective. On stone surfaces, only pH-neutral cleaners are safe — acid dissolves marble/limestone. Preventing mineral buildup is easier than removing it — squeegee/dry after water contact.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Remove loose material mechanically — brush, scrape, or vacuum as appropriate
2. Apply NSD to the stain area
3. Apply appropriate secondary agent based on stain response
4. Flush with cool water. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Mix equal parts white vinegar and water. Apply to mineral deposits
2. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat if needed
3. Rinse with clean water to remove vinegar residue
4. For fabric: launder normally. For hard surfaces: wipe dry
5. Prevention: squeegee or dry surfaces after water contact
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If water stain stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is water stain on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
